(English: Basketball Club Krka), commonly referred to as **KK Krka** or simply **Krka**, is a Slovenian professional [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") team based in [Novo Mesto](/wiki/Novo_Mesto "Novo Mesto") that competes in the [Slovenian First League](/wiki/Premier_A_Slovenian_Basketball_League "Premier A Slovenian Basketball League") and the [ABA League](/wiki/ABA_League "ABA League"). They play their home games at Leon Štukelj Hall. The club is a founding member and shareholder of the [Adriatic Basketball Association](/wiki/ABA_League_JTD "ABA League JTD").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

**Košarkarski klub Krka** (English: Basketball Club Krka), commonly referred to as **KK Krka** or simply **Krka**, is a Slovenian professional [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") team based in [Novo Mesto](/wiki/Novo_Mesto "Novo Mesto") that competes in the [Slovenian First League](/wiki/Premier_A_Slovenian_Basketball_League "Premier A Slovenian Basketball League") and the [ABA League](/wiki/ABA_League "ABA League"). They play their home games at Leon Štukelj Hall. The club is a founding member and shareholder of the [Adriatic Basketball Association](/wiki/ABA_League_JTD "ABA League JTD").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KK_Krka&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1948–2002

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KK_Krka&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1948–2002")]

The first basketball club in [Novo Mesto](/wiki/Novo_Mesto "Novo Mesto") was founded in 1948.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In 1983, the club won its first Slovenian national championship.[[3]](#cite_note-history-3) After that, however, basketball saw a decline in Novo Mesto that lasted until the Slovenian independence in 1991. Soon, the team was climbing through the lower divisions and earned a promotion to the [Slovenian First League](/wiki/UPC_Telemach_League "UPC Telemach League") in 1997, when it also took the name of its sponsor, [Krka](/wiki/Krka_(company) "Krka (company)"). A fourth-place finish in the national league allowed the club to qualify for the [Korać Cup](/wiki/Kora%C4%87_Cup "Korać Cup") for the 1998–99 season. The big breakthrough came a year later, however, as Krka defeated historical rival [Olimpija](/wiki/KK_Union_Olimpija "KK Union Olimpija") in the Slovenian championship finals to win the national title. That year, the team also played in the [Saporta Cup](/wiki/Saporta_Cup "Saporta Cup"). In 2000–01, Krka lost to Olimpija in the national league finals. In 2001–02, Krka qualified for the [EuroLeague](/wiki/EuroLeague "EuroLeague"). Though Krka missed the Top 16, it was able to defeat all its opponents once, including [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_Baloncesto "Real Madrid Baloncesto"), [Panathinaikos](/wiki/Panathinaikos_BC "Panathinaikos BC"), [CSKA Moscow](/wiki/PBC_CSKA_Moscow "PBC CSKA Moscow"), and [Skipper Bologna](/wiki/Fortitudo_Bologna "Fortitudo Bologna"). At home, Krka finished as the runners-up in the [Adriatic League](/wiki/ABA_League "ABA League"), in the Slovenian Championship, and the [national cup](/wiki/Slovenian_Basketball_Cup "Slovenian Basketball Cup"), losing to Olimpija on all three occasions.

### 2002–2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KK_Krka&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2002–2010")]

Krka zoomed to the [2003 ULEB Cup double finals](/wiki/ULEB_Cup "ULEB Cup"), narrowly losing to [Pamesa Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_BC "Valencia BC") despite being the only ULEB Cup team to win in Valencia. Krka won their second Slovenian championship, beating Olimpija in the finals.

Zoran Martić resigned during the 2003–04 season after poor results in the Euroleague, and was replaced by Petar Skansi. Krka finished the Euroleague with only two wins. In the Adriatic League and the Slovenian League, Krka finished in 7th place. A loss against [Helios Domžale](/wiki/KK_Dom%C5%BEale "KK Domžale") in the quarterfinals meant relegation from the Adriatic League. The 2005–06 season was one of the worst in the club's history after Krka finished in last place after the regular season. In the relegation group, Krka finished 9th and avoided the drop. In the 2007–08 season, Krka finished third in the domestic league and replaced [Geoplin Slovan](/wiki/KD_Slovan "KD Slovan") in the Adriatic League. Krka was one of three Slovenian clubs representing Slovenia in the regional Adriatic League in the 2008–09 season, finishing in 11th place. In 1. SKL Krka finished third.

[Aleksandar Džikić](/wiki/Aleksandar_D%C5%BEiki%C4%87 "Aleksandar Džikić") became head coach of the club for the 2009–10 season. Finishing first after the regular part of the season and second in the championship group, the team qualified for the semifinals. They then defeated Helios 2–1 in the series, reaching the finals for the first time since 2003. Krka defeated Olimpija 3–2 in the final and won the national title.

### 2010–11 season: National and Eurochallenge champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KK_Krka&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2010–11 season: National and Eurochallenge champions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Krka_olimpija_januar11.jpg/250px-Krka_olimpija_januar11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Krka_olimpija_januar11.jpg)

Eurochallenge game

Aleksandar Džikić was confirmed as head coach for the 2010–11 season. He got a new assistant coach for help, [Aleksander Sekulić](/wiki/Aleksander_Sekuli%C4%87 "Aleksander Sekulić"). Krka qualified for Adriatic League and decided to compete in the third-rank European league called [FIBA EuroChallenge](/wiki/FIBA_EuroChallenge "FIBA EuroChallenge"). The season started with a win in the [Slovenian Supercup](/wiki/Slovenian_Basketball_Supercup "Slovenian Basketball Supercup"), beating Olimpija in [Maribor](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor"). Krka qualified for Adriatic League Final 4, where they lost against Olimpija in the semifinals. They also made it to the EuroChallenge Final 4 where they won the club's first European trophy, beating [Oostende](/wiki/BC_Oostende "BC Oostende") in the semifinal and [Lokomotiv-Kuban](/wiki/PBC_Lokomotiv-Kuban "PBC Lokomotiv-Kuban") in the final. They finished the Slovenian League in first place with one loss. In the semifinals Krka defeated [Zlatorog Laško](/wiki/KK_Zlatorog_La%C5%A1ko "KK Zlatorog Laško") 2–0. The successful season ended on 11 June when Krka won the Slovenian League after beating Olimpija 3–2 in the final, thus winning their fourth national title and the second in a row.

### 2011–12 season: back to EuroCup, national champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KK_Krka&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2011–12 season: back to EuroCup, national champions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Krka_team_2012.jpg/250px-Krka_team_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Krka_team_2012.jpg)

Krka team in 2012

Krka competed in the [ABA league](/wiki/Liga_ABA "Liga ABA"), [Eurocup](/wiki/Eurocup_Basketball "Eurocup Basketball"), Slovenian Cup, and [Slovenian Telemach League](/wiki/Premier_A_Slovenian_Basketball_League "Premier A Slovenian Basketball League"). Coach Aleksandar Džikić was replaced with [Nenad Trajković](/wiki/Nenad_Trajkovi%C4%87_(basketball) "Nenad Trajković (basketball)"). [Dušan Đorđević](/wiki/Du%C5%A1an_%C4%90or%C4%91evi%C4%87_(basketball) "Dušan Đorđević (basketball)"), [Goran Ikonić](/wiki/Goran_Ikoni%C4%87 "Goran Ikonić"), [Dragiša Drobnjak](/wiki/Dragi%C5%A1a_Drobnjak "Dragiša Drobnjak") and [Chris Booker](/wiki/Chris_Booker_(basketball) "Chris Booker (basketball)") has left the club. Their replacements were [Jaka Klobučar](/wiki/Jaka_Klobu%C4%8Dar "Jaka Klobučar"), [Curtis Stinson](/wiki/Curtis_Stinson "Curtis Stinson"), Marko Đurković, [Jimmy Baxter](/wiki/Jimmy_Baxter_(basketball) "Jimmy Baxter (basketball)"), and [Jerome Jordan](/wiki/Jerome_Jordan "Jerome Jordan"). The club had two players on the Slovenian national team, [Edo Murić](/wiki/Edo_Muri%C4%87 "Edo Murić") and [Zoran Dragić](/wiki/Zoran_Dragi%C4%87 "Zoran Dragić"). Krka had successfully started a season in October, winning Slovenian Supercup over Olimpija. In November, Jaka Klobučar and Curtis Stinson left the club. Klobučar's wish was to play in a foreign country, while Stinson went to surgery (broken ankle). Krka bought Allan Ray who played only three games. After an excellent performance against Le Mans, he moved to France. His replacement was [Mustafa Abdul-Hamid](/wiki/Mustafa_Abdul-Hamid "Mustafa Abdul-Hamid").

On 1 December, coach Trajković moved to the [Phoenix Suns](/wiki/Phoenix_Suns "Phoenix Suns") and was replaced with his assistant Sekulić. Also, [Jerome Jordan](/wiki/Jerome_Jordan "Jerome Jordan") left the club (NBA-out clause), along with [Jimmy Baxter](/wiki/Jimmy_Baxter_(basketball) "Jimmy Baxter (basketball)") (denied documents) and Marko Đurković (poor performances). On 29 December Krka announced three new players, [Ben Hansbrough](/wiki/Ben_Hansbrough "Ben Hansbrough") from [Bayern München](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich_(basketball) "FC Bayern Munich (basketball)"), [Uroš Lučić](/wiki/Uro%C5%A1_Lu%C4%8Di%C4%87 "Uroš Lučić") from [Radnički Kragujevac](/wiki/KK_Radni%C4%8Dki_Kragujevac_(2009%E2%80%932014) "KK Radnički Kragujevac (2009–2014)"), who already played for Krka in the 2009–10 season and [Jure Lalić](/wiki/Jure_Lali%C4%87 "Jure Lalić"), who came from [Cibona](/wiki/KK_Cibona "KK Cibona"). In January 2012, the club released [Mustafa Abdul-Hamid](/wiki/Mustafa_Abdul-Hamid "Mustafa Abdul-Hamid"), but after one month he came back to the club in February 2012. The club also announced a new player on PG position. That became [Afik Nissim](/wiki/Afik_Nissim "Afik Nissim"). The season ended with winning their fifth national championship, the third in a row.

### 2012–13 season: return to Eurochallenge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KK_Krka&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2012–13 season: return to Eurochallenge")]

The club competed in the [ABA league](/wiki/Liga_ABA "Liga ABA"), [Slovenian Telemach League](/wiki/Premier_A_Slovenian_Basketball_League "Premier A Slovenian Basketball League"), [EuroChallenge](/wiki/EuroChallenge_2012%E2%80%9313 "EuroChallenge 2012–13") and Slovenian Cup. After winning the national trophy in the previous season, Sekulić continued as the main coach. New assistant coach became Gašper Potočnik and former Krka player [Simon Petrov](/wiki/Simon_Petrov "Simon Petrov"). Club signed Jaka Klobučar, [Jakov Vladović](/wiki/Jakov_Vladovi%C4%87 "Jakov Vladović") and [Jurica Golemac](/wiki/Jurica_Golemac "Jurica Golemac") and two youngsters, Erjon Kastrati and Tomaž Bolčina. Jure Balažić changed clubs and went to Turkey while Simon Petrov retired and became an assistant coach. Krka started the season with a Slovenian Supercup win over Olimpija (84–81) on 25 September in Grosuplje. On 30 December, [Matjaž Smodiš](/wiki/Matja%C5%BE_Smodi%C5%A1 "Matjaž Smodiš") and president Brane Kastelec announced his comeback to his home club. Due to bad results, especially defeat in the semifinals of the national cup against Helios on 11 February, main coach Sekulić was released. Gašper Potočnik became a new head coach, who was previously an assistant. In March, Krka signed combo guard [Jerime Anderson](/wiki/Jerime_Anderson "Jerime Anderson"), who played his first game in the national championship against Helios. In the championship, Krka finished the regular season in first place with only one defeat (9–1). In the semifinals, the team won 2–0 against Zlatorog Laško and advanced to the finals against Olimpija, where Krka won the series 3–1, clinching its fourth consecutive title and sixth overall. The last game had a huge contribution from Matjaž Smodiš who scored 21 points and successfully ended his career.

### 2013–14 season: Džikić comeback

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KK_Krka&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2013–14 season: Džikić comeback")]

On 18 June 2013, Krka signed a two-year contract with Aleksandar Džikić. The first team addition was Jasmin Hukić, signed on 25 June. Club added Croatian power forward [Zvonko Buljan](/wiki/Zvonko_Buljan "Zvonko Buljan") and Slovenian guard Luka Lapornik on 12 July.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/National_title_2014.jpg/250px-National_title_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_title_2014.jpg)

National title winners in 2014

On 24 July, the club announced the signing of Derrick Nix on a three-year contract, but because of his improper behavior, they terminated his contract and signed Chris Booker instead. American PG [Malcolm Armstead](/wiki/Malcolm_Armstead "Malcolm Armstead") joined the club on 30 July.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In the middle of the season, [Sani Bečirović](/wiki/Sani_Be%C4%8Dirovi%C4%87 "Sani Bečirović"), a Slovenian international, joined the team. 2013–14 was the first season in which Krka won the national cup and after winning the national championship in May 2014, Krka won their first "double crown" in the history of the club. Jaka Klobučar was awarded the Slovenian League MVP title. Krka finished in seventh place in the Adriatic League, just a spot away from directly qualifying for EuroCup.

### 2014–15 season: Cup winners, semifinals of the national championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KK_Krka&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2014–15 season: Cup winners, semifinals of the national championship")]

On 14 June, two new players were confirmed, Cameroonian [Alexis Wangmene](/wiki/Alexis_Wangmene "Alexis Wangmene") and [Mirko Mulalić](/wiki/Mirko_Mulali%C4%87 "Mirko Mulalić"), while [Smiljan Pavič](/wiki/Smiljan_Pavi%C4%8D "Smiljan Pavič"), Jaka Klobučar, Sani Bečirović, Zvonko Buljan, Marko Pajić, and Edo Murić have left the club. Aleksandar Džikić kept his place as head coach. On 26 June 2014, ULEB decided that Krka can not play in its hall, resulting in an absence from the European competitions for the first time in four years. Krka eventually played in the Slovenian and ABA league. On 9 July, Krka signed a one-year contract with former Slovenian national team player [Nebojša Joksimović](/wiki/Neboj%C5%A1a_Joksimovi%C4%87_(basketball) "Nebojša Joksimović (basketball)"). In July, Krka announced that Christopher Booker decided to come back to Novo Mesto and extended his contract. Before the start of the season, Krka won the Slovenian Supercup against Olimpija. The ABA League season started with a 5–1 record, but it was followed by nine defeats in the next ten games. In January, a new five-win streak started and in February, the team won its second national cup against Zlatorog Laško. After eight defeats in the regular season of the Slovenian League, Krka finished first in the championship round (8–2). After the quarterfinal win against [Portorož](/wiki/KK_Portoro%C5%BE "KK Portorož") in the playoffs (2–0), the team was eliminated in the semifinals by [Tajfun](/wiki/KK_%C5%A0entjur "KK Šentjur").

### 2015–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KK_Krka&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2015–present")]

On 21 June 2015, [Ivan Velić](/wiki/Ivan_Veli%C4%87 "Ivan Velić") was hired as a new head coach.[[6]](#cite_note-velic-6) [Vladimir Anzulović](/wiki/Vladimir_Anzulovi%C4%87 "Vladimir Anzulović") became a new assistant coach, previously head coach of KK Kolpa Črnomelj.[[6]](#cite_note-velic-6)

In the next few seasons, Krka struggled in the Slovenian League, getting eliminated in the semifinals in 2016 and 2017. Krka made the finals in 2018 but lost to Olimpija.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 2016, Krka also won the Slovenian Cup and Slovenian Supercup.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) Krka also struggled in the Adriatic League, and were relegated to the newly established [ABA League Second Division](/wiki/ABA_League_Second_Division "ABA League Second Division") for the 2017–18 season. In their first ABA Second Division season, Krka qualified for the [2018 ABA League Second Division Final Four](/wiki/2018_ABA_League_Second_Division_Final_Four "2018 ABA League Second Division Final Four"), which they won by beating [Borac Čačak](/wiki/KK_Borac_%C4%8Ca%C4%8Dak "KK Borac Čačak") in the semifinals and [Primorska](/wiki/KK_Primorska "KK Primorska") in the final.[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Marko Jošilo](/wiki/Marko_Jo%C5%A1ilo "Marko Jošilo") was named the MVP of the finals, and Krka was promoted back to the first Adriatic League division.

## Arena

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KK_Krka&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Arena")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/5thGameOf2011SlovenianFinalsLeonStukeljHall.jpg/250px-5thGameOf2011SlovenianFinalsLeonStukeljHall.jpg)](/wiki/File:5thGameOf2011SlovenianFinalsLeonStukeljHall.jpg)

Fifth game of the Slovenian League finals in 2011, showing Leon Štukelj Hall from the southeast side

The first ground of the club was an outdoor asphalt playground called Loka, near the river [Krka](/wiki/Krka_(Sava) "Krka (Sava)"). In the 1970s, Krka moved to the first indoor arena in [Novo Mesto](/wiki/Novo_Mesto "Novo Mesto"), named Marof. It has a capacity of 1,500 spectators. Marof was the home court of Krka for over 30 years and is still being used as a training court. The 2009–10 season playoff finals were also played in Marof.

Today, Krka play their home games at Leon Štukelj Hall with a capacity of 2,500 seats, located in the school center in the southwestern part of Novo Mesto. It is the town's primary sports gym and is the venue for many events, as it is the largest hall in Novo Mesto and [Lower Carniola](/wiki/Lower_Carniola "Lower Carniola").

A new arena with around 5,000 seats was planned for [EuroBasket 2013](/wiki/EuroBasket_2013 "EuroBasket 2013") in Slovenia,[[11]](#cite_note-11) but the project was canceled as well as Novo Mesto's bid for EuroBasket.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
